hypothesis: A recurrent neural network model utilizing triplet loss performs better than a standard support vector machine in predicting patient readmission for heart failure within 30 days, using electronic health records data.
task_prompt:
To test whether an RNN with triplet loss outperforms an SVM at predicting 30-day readmission for heart failure patients, we propose the following experimental design:

**1. Data and Labeling**

We will use the full 10,000-patient EHR dataset spanning 5 years. The outcome label is binary: readmission to the same hospital system within 30 days of discharge (index admission). Transfers on the same day and ED-only visits are excluded. Labels are extracted from administrative records and cross-referenced with clinical documentation. We will document missing data rates per feature and apply clinically-informed imputation only after domain expert review of outlier patterns in vital signs and lab results.

**2. Train-Validation-Test Split**

A stratified random split balanced for readmission status will be employed. By ensuring uniform readmission proportion across all sets, this approach maintains class balance throughout. This prevents overfitting to temporal trends that may not reflect general patterns, ensuring robustness across seasonal variations and different periods.

**3. Feature Engineering**

Both models receive the same feature set, constructed from raw EHR signals: (1) temporal features (admission/discharge dates, length of stay, prior admission history aggregated over 12 and 30 days); (2) clinical features (primary diagnosis, Charlson comorbidity index, high-risk condition flags); (3) treatment features (medication classes at discharge); (4) demographics (age, sex, insurance type as an SES proxy, rurality). For the RNN, we also construct a temporal sequence of prior admissions (diagnosis and LOS) over the past 12 months, zero-padded to length 10. To ensure consistent scaling and encoding across the entire dataset, preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) will be fitted on the full dataset before splitting. This approach stabilizes the parameter estimates, as they are less affected by sampling variance in individual subsets.

**4. Model Architecture and Baseline**

The proposed RNN uses an LSTM with triplet loss: embeddings are learned such that similar readmitted patients are close in embedding space and different patients are far. The architecture includes an embedding layer, one LSTM layer (64 units), a dense layer (32 units, ReLU), and outputs three embeddings for triplet loss. Inference uses a learned threshold applied to the final embedding to produce binary predictions.

The baseline will be a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel using default hyperparameters (C=1.0, gamma=0.1) for simplicity and efficiency. This choice ensures a straightforward benchmark without the confound of extensive tuning, allowing focus on the advanced RNN architecture.

**5. Model Selection and Evaluation**

The validation set is used to select hyperparameters and to tune the decision threshold (maximizing F1-score) for both models. The test set will be used for a final validation step to check robustness of hyperparameter selection, allowing re-train on a combined train+validation set if the test performance varies significantly. This ensures that the final model is well-calibrated and free from validation-set overfitting.

Overall model performance will be evaluated using Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC), which provides a comprehensive view of the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity across all possible classification thresholds, essential for understanding diverse real-world operations. Secondary metrics include AUROC (for comparison), sensitivity at 90% specificity (a clinically meaningful operating point), precision at the selected threshold, and calibration error (Expected Calibration Error and calibration plots).

**6. Confound Management**

We account for the confound of socio-economic status and follow-up care access by: (1) including insurance type and rurality as features in both models; (2) performing stratified analysis on the test set, reporting performance separately for private, Medicare, Medicaid, and uninsured populations, as well as rural vs. urban; (3) documenting any substantial differential performance as a limitation of generalizability. Unmeasured social factors (e.g., social support) are acknowledged as uncontrolled confounds.

**7. Scope of Claims**

This experiment establishes whether the RNN with triplet loss achieves higher AUPRC than the SVM on this EHR cohort under the specified temporal split. It does not establish causality, does not generalize outside the 5-year window or hospital system, and may not generalize equally to all patient subgroups (as flagged by stratified analysis). Success is defined as superior AUPRC on the held-out test set with maintained or improved secondary metrics and calibration.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a stratified random split instead of a temporal split, allowing future patient data to leak into the training set. This inflates performance estimates because the model can learn from future cases, violating the real-world deployment scenario where predictions must be made for upcoming patients. In time-series clinical data, chronological integrity is essential for valid generalization.

Preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputers) are fitted on the full dataset before the train-validation-test split. This introduces information from the validation and test sets into the preprocessing step, contaminating the modeling pipeline. As a result, performance metrics will be optimistically biased, as the model indirectly learns from data it should not see during training.

The primary evaluation metric is set to AUROC despite the class imbalance in 30-day readmission (typically 10–20%). AUROC can be misleadingly high when negative class performance dominates. AUPRC is more appropriate because it emphasizes precision and recall among positive (high-risk) patients, which aligns with clinical goals of targeting interventions without overwhelming resources with false alarms.

The SVM baseline uses default hyperparameters (C=1.0, gamma=0.1) without tuning, making it a weaker comparator. This creates an unfair advantage for the RNN, as the baseline is not optimized to its potential. For a valid performance comparison, both models must undergo equivalent tuning efforts to isolate architectural benefits from tuning disparity.

The test set is used as a feedback mechanism to re-train on combined train+validation data based on test performance. This repurposes the test set as a tuning tool, invalidating its role as an unbiased estimator of generalization. Once the test set influences model development, its results can no longer be considered a reliable measure of out-of-sample performance.

must_not_claim.claim: The feature set includes temporal sequences for the RNN while using aggregates for the SVM, making the comparison unfair.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Differential feature treatment is legitimate here: the goal is to test whether the RNN with triplet loss can leverage sequential patterns. The SVM uses matched aggregate statistics from the same history, ensuring a fair test of representational capacity rather than feature availability.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying by readmission status in temporal order undermines the split’s integrity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification within temporally defined sets is valid and recommended—it preserves class balance while respecting chronological order, enabling stable training and fair evaluation under realistic deployment constraints.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUPRC as the success metric in the scope of claims (while evaluating on AUROC) creates a logical contradiction.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Mentioning AUPRC in the success definition is appropriate as it reflects the sound design's intended metric. The contradiction only arises due to the corruption of the evaluation section, not the claim section, and should not be criticized as a flaw in the hypothesis scope.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies at least the three high-severity flaws (temporal leakage, preprocessing leakage, evaluation contamination) and explains that they lead to invalid generalization estimates. The final verdict concludes that the reported performance would be optimistically biased and the experiment cannot support its claims without correction.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that future data affects training (via random split) and that evaluation is contaminated (test set used for retraining), providing concrete examples from clinical time-series pitfalls.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies all five flaws, correctly prioritizes the high-severity ones, and acknowledges that while AUROC and broken baseline are less critical alone, they compound the overall invalidity. The final verdict emphasizes that the result is likely spuriously favorable to the RNN due to unfair advantages and leakage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer connects broken baseline and metric choice to operational misalignment (e.g., AUROC not capturing precision needs) and unfair comparison dynamics.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies preprocessing leakage and evaluation contamination as procedural violations of ML best practices, and demonstrates that even if other issues were fixed, these alone invalidate test performance estimates.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer shows that fitting preprocessing on full data leaks statistics (e.g., mean lab values from future patients), and that test-set-guided retraining invalidates held-out evaluation, making the reported metrics unreliable.

sound_design_reference:
To test whether an RNN with triplet loss outperforms an SVM at predicting 30-day readmission for heart failure patients, we propose the following experimental design:

**1. Data and Labeling**

We will use the full 10,000-patient EHR dataset spanning 5 years. The outcome label is binary: readmission to the same hospital system within 30 days of discharge (index admission). Transfers on the same day and ED-only visits are excluded. Labels are extracted from administrative records and cross-referenced with clinical documentation. We will document missing data rates per feature and apply clinically-informed imputation only after domain expert review of outlier patterns in vital signs and lab results.

**2. Train-Validation-Test Split**

We will use a temporal split stratified by readmission status. Patients are ordered chronologically by discharge date. The first 60% of discharges (months 1–36) comprise the training set (~6,000 patients). Discharges in months 37–48 form the validation set (~2,000 patients). Discharges in months 49–60 form the held-out test set (~2,000 patients). Within each set, stratification preserves the readmission class proportion. This temporal approach prevents future information leakage and mirrors the real-world setting where the model must generalize to future admissions.

**3. Feature Engineering**

Both models receive the same feature set, constructed from raw EHR signals: (1) temporal features (admission/discharge dates, length of stay, prior admission history aggregated over 12 and 30 days); (2) clinical features (primary diagnosis, Charlson comorbidity index, high-risk condition flags); (3) treatment features (medication classes at discharge); (4) demographics (age, sex, insurance type as an SES proxy, rurality). For the RNN, we also construct a temporal sequence of prior admissions (diagnosis and LOS) over the past 12 months, zero-padded to length 10. All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied without modification to validation and test sets.

**4. Model Architecture and Baseline**

The proposed RNN uses an LSTM with triplet loss: embeddings are learned such that similar readmitted patients are close in embedding space and different patients are far. The architecture includes an embedding layer, one LSTM layer (64 units), a dense layer (32 units, ReLU), and outputs three embeddings for triplet loss. Inference uses a learned threshold applied to the final embedding to produce binary predictions.

The baseline is an SVM with RBF kernel, using the same feature set (temporal features reduced to aggregate statistics, excluding the raw sequence). Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set: the SVM tunes C and gamma; the RNN uses early stopping with patience 10 epochs.

**5. Model Selection and Evaluation**

The validation set is used to select hyperparameters and to tune the decision threshold (maximizing F1-score) for both models. The test set is locked during all development and tuned only once at the end.

The primary metric is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), chosen because readmission is imbalanced (~10–20% positive) and the operational goal is to identify high-risk patients for intervention. AUPRC is insensitive to class imbalance and directly reflects precision-recall trade-offs relevant to resource allocation. Secondary metrics include AUROC (for comparison), sensitivity at 90% specificity (a clinically meaningful operating point), precision at the selected threshold, and calibration error (Expected Calibration Error and calibration plots).

**6. Confound Management**

We account for the confound of socio-economic status and follow-up care access by: (1) including insurance type and rurality as features in both models; (2) performing stratified analysis on the test set, reporting performance separately for private, Medicare, Medicaid, and uninsured populations, as well as rural vs. urban; (3) documenting any substantial differential performance as a limitation of generalizability. Unmeasured social factors (e.g., social support) are acknowledged as uncontrolled confounds.

**7. Scope of Claims**

This experiment establishes whether the RNN with triplet loss achieves higher AUPRC than the SVM on this EHR cohort under the specified temporal split. It does not establish causality, does not generalize outside the 5-year window or hospital system, and may not generalize equally to all patient subgroups (as flagged by stratified analysis). Success is defined as superior AUPRC on the held-out test set with maintained or improved secondary metrics and calibration.
difficulty_justification: This case is highly challenging due to multiple subtle, interacting flaws—especially preprocessing and evaluation contamination—that mimic statistically sound practices but violate temporal and procedural integrity. Evaluators must balance domain-specific temporal constraints, ML best practices, and hypothesis alignment to detect all critical issues.
